author: @gen81465
text:
I have a question for you, although it's possible it may have already been asked (as this video is a number of years old). My apologies if you've already covered this subject.

How many times can you run the same salt water over the graphene surface, and maintain the voltage?

My reasoning for this is that I saw another video of yours, where the device was sitting in seawater and as the water flowed over the device, it generated current. I was thinking that if you had a secondary battery that was used solely to power a small pump, you could enclose the device with an input pipe, and output pipe, and a container with the saltwater solution. By pumping it continuously over the device, it would keep generating current. If the system is a closed loop, how long might something like this run?

I know my idea may be crazy, but then again, I often have crazy ideas that sometimes work.

Best of luck in your endeavors.

George

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text: Wish I could purchase your books through Amazon for kindle.  Would love to support you.

author: @KimberlyRPeacock
text: Science the way it was meant to be done, by doing it, getting experimental results and then making sense of it, and doing other experiments. Bravo.  I immediately thought of lord kelvin's thunderstorm a water drop electrostatic generator.   Of course there you are dealing with Kilovolts and microamps.  Just an idea, but you could create a stack much like a cellcore stack but one which expanded and contracted based on a small temperature difference quickly. Just like is done with a Saser (Acoustic Laser). This could force water at higher velocity back and forth across the graphine. Essentially an electric generator working much like an ultrasonic piezoelectric generator. The advantage is high power density and low weight.  I will put one together, test and report back on the results. You can look at some of my older patents to see how I would use the material.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Definitely - i would be fascinated to see - thanks for the hints and help

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith  Not sure I have been of much help, but you have helped me, and hopefully I can return the favor.  I am making hydride heat engines which are powered by ambient temperature conditions.  This is possible because hydrides equilibrium pressure can double and half in 15K increments.  The rate of the reaction is proportional to how quickly you can move thermal energy in and out, and the best one are notoriously poor thermal conductors. So I've become a fancy plumber doing heat pipe heat exchangers, and thin films to get fast cycle times. Yes, I have patents and have worked on this for over 10 years and am about to hit payday, but I do understand your frustration with the current system. I also feel a bit guilty because my work is built upon everyone elses.  It seems sort of unfair sometimes that to one person or worse company goes all the credit.  Anyway the hydrides when thermally cycled have enough fluid power to run a vapor compression system which cools one hydride by allowing a refrigerant to expand, and reject the heat to the hydride releasing hydrogen undergoing the endothermic reaction.  Operating temperature from 0C to 30C.  In essence we have a sort of chemical stirling engine with a vapor compression system being the thermal battery, and as long as your rms mean temp is ambient then all the internal energy is keep constant just changing with the outside ambient within the temp range.  The hydride I am using is CeNi5 because its gut a high eq pressure at low temperatures, but others could be used in the same manner. I hope maybe this help you on something. Seeing as you have shared so much with me. I wish to reduce the mechanical parts and weight so we could power cars, boats, etc this way.  You have given me many ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Kimberly Peacock
that's good to know - when you were talking i was reminded of Einstein's fridge. I have absolutely no problem with genuine patents I have a problem with lawyers and too broad a scope that limits others too much - let me know a few numbers and i will have a read

replies.author: @KimberlyRPeacock
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith As to Einstein's refrigerator yes its an absorption type but the cool thing about the hydrides is they alternate between being heat pumps and engines and can be tuned.  To my mind thermocycling them is the best path for the technology.  I think we are kindred spirits.  People forget that Michael Faraday was self educated.  I will explore any idea as long as it can be demonstrated.  I think Feynman philosophy matches mine, in that for many problems you need to attack it from a different angle or perspective, and to do that you have to be open to surprises.  I admire you.

author: @ScottSextonandDavidLewis
text: Wow. WOW. That's terrific Robert!  I'm super delighted that I could tip you to the discovery made by the team of researchers at China's Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, and I hardly know what to say because I'm so excited at the possibilities implied by your ink here. Quick, take some sort of large clever topology surface coated with your ink to the sea shore and see what sort of potentials are possible! And do please make a video there to clue us all in on your findings. I'm trying to stay calm, but I'm so hoping the principal can be scaled to generate usable electricity. What sort of nano-amperes did you see with this first tiny scale test?

replies.author: @ScottSextonandDavidLewis
replies.text:
To better explain my excitement for new readers:

Excerpt below from the phys.org article on the discovery of salt water flowing over graphene electricity generation principal:

"Using the newly discovered technique [of salt water flowing over graphene] to generate electricity isn't going to become a commercial proposition anytime soon, of course, as there is still the tricky problem of creating mass amounts of [mono-layer] graphene at a reasonable price. But if that ever happens, people everywhere could very easily create their own electricity, as it appears the process is exceptionally scalable."

Read more at: http://phys.org/news/2014-04-team-electricity-saltwater-graphene.html#jCp

The phys.org article above was written before Robert's reasonably priced process for waterproof graphene ink may make it possible to generate cheap eco-friendly electricity.

author: @DiyEcoProjects
text: from google: “When a saltwater drop sits still on top of a strip of graphene, any charge it has is redistributed symmetrically on both sides of the droplet, leaving zero net potential difference between them. When the drop is moved, however, the distribution becomes unbalanced. The electrons from graphene are desorbed at one end of the drop and absorbed at the other. The difference gives rise to an electric potential on one side of the drop, generating a voltage across its length… The resulting electric potential is proportional to the velocity (of the dragged droplet) and the number of droplets.” ... doesnt have to be droplets, i can be water flow... LIKE IN THIS VIDEO

author: @cheerdiver
text:
In the exp, they isolated each side of the water drop.  The article implied the voltage potential was on the leading and trailing edges of the water, which seems to be needed to keep the voltage from shorting itself out via the high conductive graphene around the sides.  Coat the inside of a tube, application should be practical as an inherent symmetrical counter force is always present.  Drop enough water through to isolate +/-, connect several tubes anode to cathode to get higher voltage battery effect.
Again, I think the minerals are having a Lenz's Law effect.

If you order the soap I will have to make some more and I am a bit rushed at the moment to do that so don't worry - to be honest I have appreciated your intellectual support more,

Rob.

author: @GreenmeResearch
text:
Congratulations!

I see +2 volts and at which amps?
Imagin a large sheet put into sea-currents, creating electricity.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i need to look at this some more mate - at the moment it is really just an oddity.

replies.author: @GreenmeResearch
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith I think you are on to something here, great informative channel by the way!

I am part of the Free Energy Party (USA/UK) and found your channel through one of the DIY Graphene and DIY ultra capacitor discussions going on over there.

I also really like the soap formula you discovered, I am thinking of buying a kilogram in the upcoming months and give samples in pumpbottles around to cargarages, flowerists and every dirtyhand business and see if they are interested.

Cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Greenme Research
sounds cool mate - let me know a little bit before hand when you want the soap as I am concentrating on the ink at the moment for obvious reasons and will have to change gear a little for the soap

author: @AlternativeDIYEnergy
text: The greatest inventions appear by trying what sounds unworkable. In this Universe of ours all things are possible. Imagine a saltlic grapene generator in every rainwater downpipe in the land. Hydro generators in slow flowing streams. The list is endless. No moving parts to wear away or sieze up. You are an incredible person. I am glad I am your friend. You inspire the novice inventors like me. Thank you so very much.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: bless your heart mate - interesting idea you have there

replies.author: @BiosWars
replies.text: Dreams do come true.

author: @BinjKomisar11
text: Excellent find. Excellent work. Excellent mind. Excellent man.... The applications of this really could be endless... Good on ya and keep up the amazing work... (In 2 weeks I will be ordering some of your special graphite and a couple of your books)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and whenever you are ready - one of the great things i like about this is that great discoveries can be made by anyone

replies.author: @BinjKomisar11
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith That is true. All it take is learning, imagination/creativity, and most important (at least in my opinion) physically attempting. I read on one of the comments below about coating the hull of a boat, too cool. How about covering sea walls and docks etc. This is really cool. I eagerly look forward to seeing where you are going to take this. Peace. :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I'll probably move on to the next thing mate - I am about providing the basics and will probably leave this to more intelligent people than me to take forward if they want

replies.author: @BinjKomisar11
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Ahhhh. Very cool. Keep going....I think a lot of people are going to have fun with this one... Well, none the less, I eagerly look forward to seeing what you come up with next. I love that attitude, keep on keepin on. Peace!
